If Customer objects, Lattice will either (i) not engage the Material Subcontractor, or (ii) provide Customer with a commercially reasonable alternative solution that does not involve the objected-to subcontractor. Lattice will impose on all subcontractors obligations no less protective than those Lattice owes to Customer under this Agreement with respect to data security, confidentiality, and data protection, and Lattice remains fully liable for subcontractor performance.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 3.2: 'All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Section 3.2: 'All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The Profile explicitly requires Net-30 payment terms, but no accepted finding modified Section 3.2. The contract as signed would retain Net-60 payment terms, contradicting Customer's playbook position and creating internal incoherence with Customer's AP cycle. This coherence issue was not caught by any specialist because payment terms did not trigger severity thresholds in isolation, but retaining vendor-favorable Net-60 after accepting other customer-favorable edits creates an incomplete negotiation posture.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Profile states 'Net-30 from receipt of invoice' as the position and 'Net-60 strains working capital and is unusual for SaaS subscriptions in our size range.' Retaining Net-60 after negotiating liability caps, termination rights, and other protective provisions leaves a vendor-favorable commercial term that contradicts Customer's stated procurement standards. While not existential, this creates a $0-cost edit that directly implements a Profile requirement. The risk is operational: Customer's AP cycle is geared for Net-30; Net-60 creates working-capital strain and forces exception handling in accounts payable. As signed with accepted edits but without this payment-term correction, the contract would be internally incoherent—protective on liability and termination but permissive on cash management.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1). Payment terms are contractual; no statutory override for SaaS subscriptions.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Section 3.2: 'All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.'

coherence_findings.proposed_text: Section 3.3: 'Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: Profile requires '1% per month maximum if any late fee is included.' Section 3.3's 1.5% monthly late fee (18% annually) exceeds Customer's playbook position. No accepted finding addressed this provision. The contract as signed would impose a late fee above Customer's stated acceptable threshold, creating incoherence with Customer's negotiation standards.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Profile states '1.5% is above market for buyer-side terms. We prefer no late fee but will accept a token rate.' The 1.5% monthly rate in Section 3.3 is precisely the rate Profile identifies as excessive. While the dollar impact depends on payment delays, the principle is that Customer's playbook ceiling is 1% and the contract exceeds it. This is a straightforward implemention of a stated Profile position that was not flagged by any specialist (payment mechanics typically fall outside risk-allocation and termination domains). The materiality is reputational and procedural: signing at 1.5% after stating 1% as the maximum creates internal inconsistency in Customer's negotiation execution.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1). Late fees are contractual; Delaware has no usury statute for commercial contracts.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 8.4: 'Customer is solely responsible for Customer Data and for ensuring that its use of the Subscription Services and the content of Customer Data complies with all applicable laws, regulations, and third-party rights. Customer represents and warrants that it has all rights, consents, and authorizations necessary to make Customer Data available to Lattice for the purposes contemplated by this Agreement.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding protective-provisions-analyst-002 proposes that Customer owns deliverables from Professional Services (Section 2.5A). However, Section 8.4 imposes a broad warranty that Customer 'has all rights, consents, and authorizations necessary to make Customer Data available to Lattice.' If Professional Services deliverables are Customer-owned per the accepted edit but were created by Lattice using Lattice's pre-existing tools and methodologies, there is potential tension: does the Section 8.4 warranty extend to deliverables that Lattice created for Customer and then assigned to Customer? The accepted edit in protective-provisions-analyst-002 addresses ownership but does not carve out Lattice-created deliverables from Customer's Section 8.4 warranty. This creates a risk that Customer warrants ownership and authorization for content Lattice contributed. Recommend annotating Section 8.4 to clarify that Customer's warranty applies to Customer-provided data and content, but not to deliverables created by Lattice under Professional Services and assigned to Customer (where Lattice warrants it had the right to assign).
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The Section 8.4 warranty as written is overbroad if read literally after accepting protective-provisions-analyst-002's ownership assignment. If Lattice creates a deliverable for Customer using a third-party library or template (not disclosed to Customer), assigns ownership to Customer, and Customer later uses that deliverable, a third party could claim infringement. Under Section 8.4's current framing, Customer warranted it 'has all rights... necessary,' which could negate Lattice's Section 8.3 IP indemnity for the deliverable (Section 8.3 excludes 'Customer Data' from indemnity coverage, and if deliverables are Customer-owned, Lattice may argue they fall within the Customer Data exclusion). The materiality is that accepting the ownership edit without conforming the Section 8.4 warranty shifts IP risk from Lattice to Customer for Lattice-created content. The harm is potential third-party IP claims against Customer for deliverables Customer did not originate. This is a classic coherence issue: an accepted edit in Section 2.5A changes the IP ownership balance, but Section 8.4's warranty language was drafted assuming Lattice retains deliverable ownership and was not updated to reflect the new allocation.
coherence_findings.position: Clarify that Customer's Section 8.4 warranty applies to Customer-provided data and content, not to deliverables created by Lattice and assigned to Customer under Section 2.5A. Lattice should warrant that assigned deliverables do not infringe third-party IP.
coherence_findings.fallback: Add a mutual representation in Section 2.5A that each Party warrants it has the right to grant the licenses and assignments it grants under that section, and that such grants do not infringe third-party IP.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1). IP warranty and indemnity allocation is contractual; Delaware enforces as written.

coherence_findings.proposed_text: Section 5.6 Data Deletion After Termination: 'Notwithstanding Section 4.4(c), if Customer Data includes personal data subject to data protection laws requiring Customer to delete or return personal data upon termination of processing, Lattice will, at Customer's election, either (a) return all such personal data to Customer in a commercially reasonable format, or (b) delete all such personal data, and in either case will certify in writing to Customer that it has done so, all within thirty (30) days of termination and in accordance with the Data Processing Addendum.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: Section 4.4(c) provides a 30-day data retrieval window after termination, after which 'Lattice may delete or destroy Customer Data.' Section 5.4 references a Data Processing Addendum (DPA) covering personal data obligations. Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes a super-cap for data breaches involving personal data, acknowledging that Customer Data may include personal data. However, no provision in the four corners requires Lattice to delete personal data post-termination in compliance with data protection laws (e.g., GDPR Article 28(3)(g) processor obligation to delete or return personal data on termination of processing). The current Section 4.4(c) is permissive ('Lattice may delete') rather than mandatory. If Customer is a data controller subject to GDPR or similar laws, Customer needs certainty that Lattice will delete or return personal data and certify deletion. This is a coherence issue: the contract references a DPA (Section 5.4) and contemplates personal data (Section 5.3 Security Incident definition, accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001's super-cap), but the termination data-handling provision (Section 4.4(c)) does not align with the data-protection framework. We propose adding Section 5.6 to close this gap.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer is subject to GDPR or CCPA and uses the Subscription Services to process personal data, Customer has a legal obligation to ensure its processor (Lattice) deletes or returns personal data on termination of processing. Section 4.4(c)'s 'may delete' language does not satisfy this obligation. The risk is regulatory: if Lattice retains personal data beyond the retrieval window and a breach occurs, Customer faces regulatory exposure for failure to ensure processor compliance. The accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 addresses liability for breaches during the term but does not address post-termination data retention. The materiality is that Customer cannot meet its data-controller obligations under the contract as currently drafted post-accepted-edits. The DPA referenced in Section 5.4 likely contains this obligation, but the MSA's termination section should align with or cross-reference the DPA's data-deletion obligations to ensure internal coherence. This was missed because compliance/data-privacy specialist findings were not present in the accepted findings (likely because the compliance module is enabled but the specialist did not emit findings, or findings were rejected for reasons not disclosed). The coherence-checker is catching the gap between the data-protection framing (Security Incidents, DPA reference, personal data super-cap) and the termination data-handling mechanics.
coherence_findings.position: Mandatory deletion or return of personal data within 30 days of termination, with written certification, in accordance with DPA obligations.
coherence_findings.fallback: Mandatory deletion or return within 60 days, or upon Customer's written request.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware governing law, but data protection obligations are informed by GDPR (extraterritorial application if EU personal data is processed) and multi-state U.S. privacy laws (CCPA, etc.).
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Section 5.6 Data Deletion After Termination: 'Notwithstanding Section 4.4(c), if Customer Data includes personal data subject to data protection laws requiring Customer to delete or return personal data upon termination of processing, Lattice will, at Customer's election, either (a) return all such personal data to Customer in a commercially reasonable format, or (b) delete all such personal data, and in either case will certify in writing to Customer that it has done so, all within thirty (30) days of termination and in accordance with the Data Processing Addendum.'
